query: FAA proposed rule supersonic overland flight July 2026 duty rest requirements transport category airplane certification streamlining emergency airworthiness directive MD-10 DC-10 2026

mako.raw:
{"query":"FAA proposed rule supersonic overland flight July 2026 duty rest requirements transport category airplane certification streamlining emergency airworthiness directive MD-10 DC-10 2026","model":"mako","response_time_ms":1896,"verdict":null,"extra_info":null,"results":[{"url":"https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/2026/07/02/2026-13440/enabling-supersonic-overland-flight","title":"Federal Register :: Enabling Supersonic Overland Flight","content":"Department of Transportation (DOT), 1200 New Jersey Avenue SE, Room W12-140, West Building Ground Floor, Washington, DC 20590-0001.
14304 in § 2(a), is “to repeal the prohibition on overland supersonic flight” in § 91.817, modify § 91.818 as necessary, and establish an interim noise-based certification standard consistent with applicable law.
Proposed § 91.817 would create new paragraphs enabling civil supersonic flight.
These requirements would be relocated as new § 91.817(c)(2).
Newly certificated transport category turbojet airplanes are now required to comply with Stage 5 noise limits."},{"url":"https://www.bdlaw.com/publications/faa-proposes-to-end-ban-on-overland-supersonic-flights-establish-interim-noise-based-en-route-standard-as-first-step-to-fully-authorizing-supersonic-operations/","title":"FAA Proposes to End Ban on Overland Supersonic Flights, Establish Interim Noise-Based En Route Standard as First Step to Fully Authorizing Supersonic Operations","content":"On July 2, 2026, the FAA proposed to remove its ban on overland supersonic flight because it is “outdated,” “no longer appropriate,” and “unnecessary,” given technology advancements in flight techniques that prevent sonic booms from reaching the surface, and “increased interest in civil supersonic flight.” FAA’s action is in response to Executive Order (EO) 14304, Leading the World in Supersonic Flight.","date":"4 days ago"},{"url":"https://www.hklaw.com/en/insights/publications/2026/07/enabling-supersonic-flight-over-land-will-performance-based","title":"Enabling Supersonic Flight Over Land: Will Performance-Based Standards Meet the Challenge? | Insights | Holland & Knight","content":"The Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) published a notice of proposed rulemaking (NPRM) on July 2, 2026, to enable supersonic flight over land as long as the operation does not exceed a certain noise standard.
Perhaps the most notable rule that indicated the FAA's commencement of an approach favoring use of performance-based standards was the agency's 2016 rulemaking to update the airworthiness standards that apply to certain types of airplanes under 14 C.F.R."},{"url":"https://www.crowell.com/en/insights/client-alerts/a-busy-week-for-aviation-regulatory-developments","title":"FAA, DOT & TSA Regulatory Roundup: Major Aviation Developments — July 2026 | Crowell & Moring LLP","content":"The 2026 UDP Rule makes several notable changes to hearing procedures:
Also on July 1, 2026, the DOT issued a notice of proposed rulemaking on Enhancing Flexibility of Air Fare Price Advertising.
The proposed changes aim to give airlines greater flexibility in how they display air fares, while ensuring that information is still presented clearly and accurately to consumers.
Notably, the DOT is considering repealing the full fare advertising rule, 14 CFR 399.84, in its entirety.
The FAA proposes to clarify that FAA regulations governing flightcrew member and flight attendant duty and rest periods preempt all state and local meal and rest break requirements per the Airline Deregulation Act of 1978.
The proposed rule would replace the long-standing U.S. ban on civil supersonic flight over land with a performance-based framework.
On June 30, 2026, the FAA published a notice of solicitation of participants for the Radar Pilot Data Program."},{"url":"https://www.transportation.gov/regulations/federal-register-documents/2026-13205","title":"Airworthiness Directives; The Boeing Company Airplanes | US Department of Transportation","content":"The FAA is adopting a new airworthiness directive (AD) for all The Boeing Company Model 707, 717, and 727 airplanes; Model DC-8, DC-9, and DC-10 airplanes; Model MD-10 and MD-11 airplanes; Model DC-9-81 (MD-81), DC-9-82 (MD-82), DC-9-83 (MD-83), DC-9-87 (MD-87), and MD-88 airplanes; and Model MD 90-30 airplanes."},{"url":"https://drs.faa.gov/browse/excelExternalWindow/FR-ADFRAWD-2026-13205-0000000000.0001","title":"DRS-2026-13-15","content":"SUMMARY: The FAA is adopting a new airworthiness directive (AD) for all The Boeing Company Model 707, 717, and 727 airplanes; Model DC-8, DC-9, and DC-10 ...Read more"}]}

tavily.raw: {"query":"FAA proposed rule supersonic overland flight July 2026 duty rest requirements transport category airplane certification streamlining emergency airworthiness directive MD-10 DC-10 2026","follow_up_questions":null,"answer":null,"images":[],"results":[{"url":"https://www.facebook.com/AeronewsGlobal/posts/faas-proposed-rule-set-to-be-finalized-by-mid-2027-sets-a-noise-based-certificat/1424376173055751","title":"FAA's proposed rule, set to be finalized by mid-2027 ...","content":"FAA's proposed rule, set to be finalized by mid-2027, sets a noise-based certification standard for supersonic aircraft.","score":0.66308063,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://drs.faa.gov/browse/excelExternalWindow/FR-ADFRAWD-2026-13205-0000000000.0001","title":"DRS-2026-13-15","content":"SUMMARY: The FAA is adopting a new airworthiness directive (AD) for all The Boeing Company Model 707, 717, and 727 airplanes; Model DC-8, DC-9, and DC-10","score":0.5835125,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.spikeaerospace.com/a-new-era-for-supersonic-flight-begins","title":"A New Era for Supersonic Flight Begins - Spike Aerospace","content":"The Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) has published a **Notice of Proposed Rulemaking (NPRM), *Enabling Supersonic Overland Flight***, proposing the first significant modernization of U.S. regulations governing civil supersonic flight in more than fifty years. Rather than relying solely on a blanket prohibition based on speed, the proposal would begin shifting toward a performance-based framework that recognizes advances in aircraft technology while continuing to protect the communities beneath future flight paths. For Spike Aerospace, this milestone is more than a regulatory development—it represents meaningful progress toward a vision that has guided our company since its founding. The **Spike S-512 Diplomat** was conceived around a simple idea: dramatically reducing travel times while designing an aircraft that addresses the environmental, safety, and community concerns that have historically limited overland supersonic flight. Designed to cruise at approximately **Mach 1.6**, the S-512 is intended to cut travel times nearly in half on many long-distance routes while pursuing a future in which high-speed flight can coexist with the communities below.","score":0.5715041,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.crowell.com/en/insights/client-alerts/a-busy-week-for-aviation-regulatory-developments","title":"A Busy Week for Aviation Regulatory Developments","content":"The proposed rule would replace the long-standing U.S. ban on civil supersonic flight over land with a performance-based framework.","score":0.5194,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.bdlaw.com/publications/faa-proposes-to-end-ban-on-overland-supersonic-flights-establish-interim-noise-based-en-route-standard-as-first-step-to-fully-authorizing-supersonic-operations","title":"FAA Proposes to End Ban on Overland Supersonic Flights, Establish Interim Noise-Based En Route Standard as First Step to Fully Authorizing Supersonic Operations","content":"# FAA Proposes to End Ban on Overland Supersonic Flights, Establish Interim Noise-Based En Route Standard as First Step to Fully Authorizing Supersonic Operations. §91.817) prohibit operation of civil aircraft at a speed greater than Mach 1 overland in the U.S. unless the operator holds an SFA (obtained pursuant to 14 C.F.R. FAA proposes to amend §91.817 to remove this general ban and instead allow supersonic flights overland in the U.S. where the operator can demonstrate, ***and*** the Administrator finds, that sonic booms reaching the surface have an overpressure of less than or equal to 0.11 psf. FAA also proposes to retain features of its existing regulations that would continue to allow supersonic flights, even where they create sonic booms, with overpressures that reach the surface and exceed the 0.11 psf standard. Importantly, FAA states that this is an interim standard and indicates it will continue to work with ICAO/CAEP to develop an international en route standard for supersonics and, once ICAO adopts such a standard, consider adopting that standard into U.S. regulation through a future rulemaking.","score":0.46380913,"raw_content":null}],"response_time":1.74,"request_id":"49db1b46-cf94-4b33-b97c-743889227ad0"}
